findings.description: reachable() enqueues every neighbor with zero visited-guard (no 'if neighbor not in seen' before append), so on a 'possibly cyclic' graph — which the docstring explicitly admits — the queue never drains and BFS runs forever / OOMs the deque; even on an acyclic diamond-shaped DAG each shared node is re-enqueued and re-expanded, degrading to exponential work instead of O(V+E).

count_paths() recurses with no memoization, so a diamond/lattice DAG re-walks identical subgraphs and blows up to O(2^n) — textbook 'Fibonacci without a cache'; a dict[(node)] -> count memo on dst collapses it to O(V+E).

count_paths() uses unbounded native Python recursion (one stack frame per graph depth), so a long dependency chain pays the per-frame call overhead and hits RecursionError ~1000 deep — an explicit stack or the memoized iterative form avoids both the cost and the crash.

Read paths self._edges[node] on a defaultdict(list) silently insert an empty-list entry for every leaf node touched (here and at lines 40, 47, 54, 61), permanently growing _edges with junk keys across calls; use self._edges.get(node, ()) on read paths to avoid the unbounded dict growth.

topological_order() rebuilds the full indegree map from scratch on every call (O(V+E) each invocation) with no caching despite the graph being mutated only via add_edge/add_node; if called repeatedly on a static graph the recomputation is pure waste, though acceptable if topo order is needed once.
